entries.title: Justice Department can no longer pressure Facebook, Apple to remove ICE-sighting apps, judge rules
entries.summary: <p>A federal judge has ordered the Justice Department and U.S. Department of Homeland Security not to pressure Facebook and Apple from banning ICE-monitoring apps and social media groups.</p><p>The preliminary injunction ruling, issued last week by U.S. District Judge Jorge Alonso, stems from <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/12/chicago-area-resident-lawsuit-facebook-ice-tracking-app" target="_blank" >a lawsuit filed in February by Kassandra “Kae” Rosado</a> against former U.S. Attorney General Pam Bondi and U.S. Secretary of Homeland Security Kristi Noem.</p><p>Rosado alleges that both federal agencies coerced Facebook into removing her “ICE Sighting-Chicagoland” group, which aimed to keep residents informed about where U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents were seen in the Chicago area.</p><p>Mark Hodges, an Indiana resident who created the mobile app Eyes Up, which allowed users to record, securely store and view videos of ICE officers committing potential civil rights violations, also joined the lawsuit. The lawsuit claims Apple removed Hodges’ app from the App Store in October — during the height of the Trump administration’s “Operation Midway Blitz” deportation campaign — after pressure from the federal government.</p><p>The judge’s order says Justice Department and DHS officials can no longer take actions to demand or pressure Facebook to suppress Rosado’s Facebook group or pressure Apple to suppress Hodges’ app.</p><p>The Justice Department and DHS were given until Tuesday to notify its employees, Facebook’s parent company Meta, and Apple of the order, and to file a status report documenting the agencies’ actions to comply.</p><p>Rosado’s group had nearly 100,000 members in October and had thousands of posts and comments per day about immigration-related arrests and violent interactions with immigration agents across the Chicago area.</p><p>On Oct. 12, right-wing activist and Trump ally Laura Loomer <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/LauraLoomer/status/1977345952718438707" target="_blank" >tagged</a> the Justice Department and DHS leaders in a post claiming that the Facebook page and others like it “are getting people killed.” The next day, Loomer said in a post that a Justice Department source told her that they had contacted executives at Meta, “to tell them they need to remove these ICE tracking pages from the platform.”</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/a8/31/3c4137a74795885492c17aa7137c/show-temp-14.pdf" target="_blank" ><i>Read the preliminary injunction</i></a><i>:</i></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe src="https://docs.google.com/viewer?embedded=true&url=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/a8/31/3c4137a74795885492c17aa7137c/show-temp-14.pdf"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: Man’s bid for a retrial in boy’s murder hinges on ex-Chicago cop’s alleged ties to Gitmo torture
entries.summary: <p>A man who says Chicago police beat him into confessing to the 1992 murder of 7-year-old Dantrell Davis is a step closer to finding out whether he’ll get a new trial.</p><p>One of Anthony Garrett’s attorneys battled a Cook County prosecutor in closing arguments Monday afternoon at the Leighton Criminal Courthouse.</p><p>The attorney, Jennifer Blagg, argued that retired Detective Richard Zuley, 79, coordinated her client's torture. She tied the case to alleged <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/04/13/settlement-finance-committee-richard-zuley-chicago-detective-carl-reed-coerced-confession" target="_blank" >Zuley-led coercion</a> in a string of cases from 1987 to 2003, including four murder convictions that were later thrown out.</p><p>“He’s never in the room when the worst stuff happens, but he doesn’t have to be [because the officers] are all working together,” Blagg said.</p><p>She also likened Garrett’s case to torture that happened when Zuley was a U.S. Naval reservist at Guantánamo Bay, Cuba. She recounted November testimony by Mohamedou Ould Slahi, 55, who appeared via Zoom from Rotterdam, the Dutch city.</p><p>Slahi, a Sept. 11 terror suspect, alleged that Zuley subjected him to months of torture in 2003 at the notorious detention camp at Guantánamo.</p><p>Slahi wrote a memoir about the experience that formed the basis of a 2021 film drama starring Jodie Foster and Benedict Cumberbatch.</p><p>“The real Dick Zuley, I would submit to the court, is the one Mohamedou Ould Slahi saw,” Blagg said Monday.</p><p>But the prosecutor, Armando G. Sandoval, urged Judge Adrienne E. Davis to disregard claims against Zuley in other cases, calling them “allegations, not findings,” and saying they “have no connection to Garrett.”</p><p>“I’m not saying everything Slahi says is false,” Sandoval said. “But the court should be careful of treating the book … or the movie as objective.”</p><p>Sandoval also cast doubt on claims by Garrett that two large men wearing sports jerseys beat him at Zuley’s behest while he was shackled to an eyebolt, and that the two men focused many of their blows on a part of his leg where physicians had implanted a rod after a gunshot wound.</p><p>The prosecutor pointed to testimony that Garrett, after his interrogation, was not limping and did not seem to be injured.</p><p>Sandoval also noted Garrett’s initial claim that the beating took place with a rubber hose. The story changed years later, Sandoval said, when Garrett claimed it was carried out with the hose and a phone book.</p><p>“His account shifts,” Sandoval said. “That’s important.”</p><p>Dantrell was killed on Oct. 13, 1992, as he walked to school with his mother from Cabrini-Green, a former North Side public housing complex. Police said a sniper shot him from an upper floor of a building.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-3a0000" name="image-3a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" alt="Dantrell Davis" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c19f4b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1399x785+0+105/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F38%2Fad%2F1f3f97414f1bbc4c375fd94bea00%2Fimage-7.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cb81a88/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1399x785+0+105/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F38%2Fad%2F1f3f97414f1bbc4c375fd94bea00%2Fimage-7.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c19f4b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1399x785+0+105/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F38%2Fad%2F1f3f97414f1bbc4c375fd94bea00%2Fimage-7.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Dantrell Davis</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Sun-Times file photo</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>A jury convicted Garrett in 1994. Now 67, he’s imprisoned at downstate Centralia Correctional Center. He’s not scheduled for release until 2039, according to the Illinois Department of Corrections.</p><p>Monday’s proceedings stem from a 2023 referral by the Illinois Torture and Relief Commission that detailed an “overwhelming” history of “lengthy and consistent” complaints alleging psychological and physical torture involving Zuley. He was hired by CPD in 1970 and worked more than three decades for the department.</p><p>Slahi, who knew Zuley as Capt. Collins, testified the detective led <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/public-safety/2025/11/25/mohamedou-ould-slahi-guantanamo-chicago-police-richard-zuley-anthony-garrett-torture-dantrell-davis" target="_blank" >“enhanced interrogation techniques,”</a> including isolation, temperature extremes, beatings, sexual humiliation, sleep deprivation, waterboarding, barking dogs, a mock execution at sea, and round-the-clock mistreatment with strobe lights and a looped recording of the U.S. national anthem.</p><p>After 70 days of torture, Slahi testified, the detective convinced him that U.S. authorities were allowing his mother to be kidnapped and raped. Slahi admitted he never saw Zuley lay a hand on him but added that he wouldn’t know because he was usually blindfolded.</p><p>Slahi testified he eventually confessed to anything his interrogators fed him, starting with plans to attack Toronto’s CN Tower. He said he also falsely confirmed that many individuals had Al Qaeda links.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/04/08/ex-gitmo-detainee-could-air-allegations-of-torture-by-former-chicago-detective" target="_blank" >Slahi was held at Guantánamo</a> for 14 years without charges before he was released to his native Mauritania in 2016. His memoirs about those years were published as a book that led to the film, “The Mauritanian,” directed by Kevin Macdonald.</p><p>At a November hearing, a prosecutor pressed Slahi about visits to Afghanistan in the early 1990s. Slahi answered he was working for U.S. allies there and cut Al Qaeda ties years before the Sept. 11 terrorist attacks.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/02/19/ex-chicago-det-richard-zuley-denies-torturing-out-confession-to-7-year-old-dantrell-daviss-murder" target="_blank" >Zuley testified</a> at Feb. 18 and April 1 hearings in Garrett’s case. The former detective said he didn’t know of anyone at the police station handcuffing Garrett to the eyebolt or subjecting him to any physical or mental abuse.</p><p>Zuley testified he left other detectives in charge of Garrett overnight. The next morning, the former detective said, Garrett said he had fired at members of a rival gang near Dantrell. Garrett later signed a handwritten confession.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a70000" name="image-a70000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5aee1be/2147483647/strip/true/crop/632x355+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbc%2Ffd%2Fec8693cb405a8749cacc3501ea56%2Fscreenshot-2025-04-07-at-10-18-19-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d4ba8a4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/632x355+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbc%2Ffd%2Fec8693cb405a8749cacc3501ea56%2Fscreenshot-2025-04-07-at-10-18-19-pm.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1c2a5e8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/632x355+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbc%2Ffd%2Fec8693cb405a8749cacc3501ea56%2Fscreenshot-2025-04-07-at-10-18-19-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Anthony Garrett is imprisoned at Centralia Correctional Center. Illinois Department of Corrections" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1c2a5e8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/632x355+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbc%2Ffd%2Fec8693cb405a8749cacc3501ea56%2Fscreenshot-2025-04-07-at-10-18-19-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/981225d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/632x355+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbc%2Ffd%2Fec8693cb405a8749cacc3501ea56%2Fscreenshot-2025-04-07-at-10-18-19-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1c2a5e8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/632x355+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbc%2Ffd%2Fec8693cb405a8749cacc3501ea56%2Fscreenshot-2025-04-07-at-10-18-19-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Anthony Garrett is imprisoned at Centralia Correctional Center. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Illinois Department of Corrections</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Threats and physical abuse, Zuley testified, were less reliable than “rapport building” for extracting information from suspects.</p><p>Judges have vacated at least four murder convictions involving Zuley. The exonerees are Lathierial Boyd, Lee Harris, Carl Reed and David Wright.</p><p>President Donald Trump’s administration blocked Garrett’s attorneys from questioning Zuley about his work at Guantánamo.</p><p>Judge Davis set the next hearing for July 14. She didn't say when she would rule on Garrett’s petition for a retrial.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/public-safety/2025/11/25/mohamedou-ould-slahi-guantanamo-chicago-police-richard-zuley-anthony-garrett-torture-dantrell-davis" target="_blank" >Ex-Guantánamo detainee testifies a former Chicago detective tortured him for months</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/02/19/ex-chicago-det-richard-zuley-denies-torturing-out-confession-to-7-year-old-dantrell-daviss-murder" target="_blank" >Ex-CPD detective denies torturing confession to Dantrell Davis murder</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/04/13/settlement-finance-committee-richard-zuley-chicago-detective-carl-reed-coerced-confession" target="_blank" >Taxpayers face $9.5M settlement tied to alleged coerced confession by onetime CPD detective</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Chip Mitchell | WBEZ

entries.title: Sky face first true playoff-caliber test against Valkyries
entries.summary: <p>SAN FRANCISCO — Beating the Fire in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/05/09/sky-survive-first-road-test-against-fire" >the regular-season opener </a>was a good sign for the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a>.</p><p>Sure, the Fire are an expansion franchise that won't sniff the playoffs this season. But you don't scoff at any wins if you're the Sky. Not when you scraped together only 10 of them last year. Not when four of your top eight players were watching in street clothes.</p><p>You can earn a few stripes beating a bad team short-handed.</p><p>But you really earn your "good-team" credentials by beating other good teams.</p><p>The Sky get their shot Wednesday night against the Valkyries. Another sellout crowd, another hostile environment. The first real chance to prove they belong in the playoff conversation.</p><p>Plenty to worry about until then.</p><p>The second half against the Fire flashed a few warning signs. Shooters left wide open in transition. Point guards dribbling the length of the floor without anyone stepping up to stop them.</p><p>At practice Monday, coach Tyler Marsh called it "coverage slippage" that needed to be cleaned up.</p><p>It will need to be squeaky clean against the Valkyries.</p><p>Golden State is well-coached, on a mission, and dangerous at every position. Their backup center, Janelle Salaun, makes it rain from deep in ways most Sky starters don't. Guards Veronica Burton and Gabby Williams make a living getting into the paint.</p><p>"A lot of their offense is based on engaging two," Natasha Cloud told the Sun-Times. "Coming off ball screens, engaging two defenders and they're really great at [making the extra pass]. As much as we can, we've got to guard our yard and take pride in our individual defense so that we're not coming into our secondary and third level of help."</p><p>Having a versatile defender like Cloud back will certainly help. But the Valkyries’ offense challenges the exact two weak points of the Sky's defense last season: containing dribble penetration and defending the arc. Time to show that this year is different.</p><p>Sky players and coaches keep pointing to the athleticism, quickness, and length on the new roster as reasons to believe they can be better defensively. Those are solid ingredients, but they won’t come together without a recipe. Does Marsh have one?</p><p>Wednesday will give us a clue.</p><p>For now, there's enough from the Fire game for Marsh to feel good about.</p><p>The offense looked cohesive. The Skylar Diggins-Kamilla Cardoso pick-and-roll was cooking.</p><p>And when the Sky looked like they were about to throw away a lead — like they did so often last season — they said, no, actually, we're not doing that. Not this time.</p><p>This year, they have Diggins' leadership to fall back on. Someone to step up and hit a big fourth-quarter three — not even her specialty — then throw her head back, stick her tongue out, let the crowd know the party is over.</p><p>The Sky also got a clutch performance from veteran center Elizabeth Williams, who came off the bench to deliver blocks and buckets when it mattered most.</p><p>Those are foundational elements to build on.</p><p>"We got to come with the same level of focus and be able to clean up the areas that we need to, but also stay true to the things that we did well coming out of Portland," Marsh said.</p><p>Clean it up and stay true. The Valkyries are waiting.</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a entries.title: RJ Day, son of Ohio State coach Ryan Day, commits to Northwestern
entries.summary: <p>Quarterback RJ Day said he is committing to play at Northwestern beginning in 2027, putting him in line to join his father in the Big Ten Conference.</p><p>The son of Ohio State football coach Ryan Day announced his decision this weekend on social media.</p><p>"Excited for the next step and ready to get to work," Day posted in a message thanking coaches and family.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Super excited to announce my commitment to further my football and academic career at Northwestern University!! Thank you to everyone along my journey who has made this possible from my family, <a href="https://twitter.com/BradMaendler?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">@BradMaendler</a> , the Desales coaching staff and Jay Hooten at the Armory. Excited for… <a href="https://t.co/3hLTnjsdSf">pic.twitter.com/3hLTnjsdSf</a></p>— RJ Day (@rj_day1) <a href="https://twitter.com/rj_day1/status/2053476361520468049?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">May 10, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Chip Kelly is the offensive coordinator on coach David Braun's staff at Northwestern after serving in that role under Day when the Buckeyes won the national championship in the 2024 season. Kelly was also the offensive coordinator when Ryan Day played QB at New Hampshire and set nine school records.</p><p>RJ Day plays at St. Francis DeSales High School in Columbus, Ohio.</p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: White Sox have come a long way, baby
entries.summary: <p>At the quarter mark of the 2026 season, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a> are easily in the best shape they have been in the standings in four years.</p><p>There was nowhere to go but up after three consecutive seasons with 100 or more losses. You can measure the Sox’ improvement by the numbers, not just the vibe.</p><p>Forty games into the 162-game schedule, the Sox are 19-21. It’s their best 40-game start since 2022, when they were 20-20 on May 21 after taking the first game of a doubleheader against the Yankees.</p><p>That .500 record foreshadowed an 81-81 season that started a rapid disintegration of a team that was supposed to do big things.</p><p>Here’s where the Sox stand after 40 games and where they stood in each of the last three painful seasons:</p><p>• 2026: 19-21, 171 runs scored, 185 runs allowed. Final record: ?</p><p>• 2025: 11-29, 135 runs scored, 172 runs allowed. Final record: 60-102.</p><p>• 2024: 12-28, 119 runs scored, 202 runs allowed. Final record 41-121, setting a modern-era record for losses.</p><p>• 2023: 13-27, 169 runs scored, 241 runs allowed. Final record: 61-101.</p><p>‘‘If you look at it on a daily basis, on a nightly basis, we feel like we have a chance to win,’’ general manager Chris Getz said at the start of the Sox’ homestand. ‘‘In the past, it was more [that] you almost needed to be perfect.</p><p>‘‘Now the lineup is deeper. You know the rotation is deeper. We’ve got some weapons in the bullpen that will allow us to navigate and finish off games. And the defense is improved.’’</p><p>A marquee slugger in Munetaka Murakami has made a difference. Big-presence batters who flirt with the major-league home-run lead not only generate deep drives and headlines, they also change the dynamics of a lineup.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/05/11/cubs-seiya-suzuki-counsels-white-sox-munetaka-murakami-kazuma-okamoto-shohei-ohtani" >Cubs’ Seiya Suzuki counsels White Sox’ Munetaka Murakami about life in Chicago</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Second-year shortstop Colson Montgomery hopes to take advantage of it as he continues to adjust to major-league pitching.</p><p>‘‘It’s completely different,’’ Montgomery said before the Sox’ 2-1, come-from-behind victory Sunday against the Mariners. ‘‘We have guys at the end of our lineup who get on base a lot. We have more guys able to put the ball in the seats and hit home runs. I think it’s all really good.’’</p><p>Beyond Murakami, Montgomery points to third baseman Miguel Vargas, who has been striking the ball with authority more consistently.</p><p>‘‘Yeah, I think me, Mune and Vargas are all really good at [being run-producers],’’ Montgomery said. ‘‘It just feels like every single time I’m up to bat, either Vargas or Mune, they are on base. There’s opportunities to drive guys in.’’</p><p>Despite injuries, such as to catcher Kyle Teel and outfielder Everson Pereira, the Sox’ offense is at its best through 40 games since 2022. They were an AL-leading 25-15 and had scored 216 runs at this point en route to winning the AL Central at 93-69 in 2021.</p><p>Montgomery is hitting only .221, but he has 10 homers and 28 RBI through 40 games. Murakami has 15 homers and 29 RBI with a .232 average and, as expected, 60 strikeouts in 173 plate appearances. Vargas is batting .230 with nine homers and 22 RBI.</p><p>But don’t let the low averages be a guide, Montgomery said.</p><p>‘‘I don’t look at my average; I don’t,’’ Montgomery said. ‘‘I know a lot of people are like, ‘They only hit this amount.’ But I just look at production and on-base percentage, OPS, things like that. Those things are what’s going to help you win games.’’</p><p>The Sox’ .231 batting average is 25th in the majors and 13th in the AL. Their run production, however, isn’t as low: 18th in MLB and ninth in the AL.</p><p>Then there are the intangibles. The Sox have been upbeat, not moping their way to the bottom. ‘‘A young, aggressive team having fun’’ seems to be a common theme.</p><p>‘‘We’re looking for winning-type players that are focused on, you know, ‘What am I going to do to help this team win?’ ’’ Getz said. ‘‘And that’s starting to seep into this organization as a whole.’’</p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" entries.author: Matt Carlson

entries.title: Man who helped grad student stabbed downtown tells jury the alleged killer later pulled a knife on him
entries.summary: <p>A man who ran to help visiting graduate student Anat Kimchi after she was fatally stabbed in a random attack downtown in June 2021 testified Monday that he saw defendant Tony Robinson attack her and that Robinson later threatened him with a knife.</p><p>The witness, Tavon Jones, said he was homeless at the time. He told the court that he had previously seen Robinson, who lived in a tent on Lower Wacker Drive, walking around the area.</p><p>Robinson, 45, is charged with first-degree murder in Kimchi's killing.</p><p>The 31-year-old University of Maryland graduate student was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2021/6/26/22551535/tony-robinson-anat-kimchi-charged-fatally-stabbing-maryland-grad-student-in-the-loop" target="_blank" >killed on a sunny Saturday afternoon</a> on June 19, 2021, near Wacker Drive and Van Buren Street, a short distance from Willis Tower.</p><p>Kimchi's murder was one of several shocking cases examined in a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/chicago-downtown-attacks-mental-health-system" target="_blank" ><u>Chicago Sun-Times investigation</u></a> last year into violent attacks in the downtown area.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-280000" name="image-280000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" alt="Anat Kimchi, a University of Maryland graduate student, was stabbed to death in 2021 on a trip to Chicago near Lower Wacker Drive where Tony Robinson, who's charged with killing her, lived in a tent, police said." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f67c119/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1828x1026+0+157/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc8%2F8a%2F7f2bdd53f8d86fe2f2d7fcdc5ec5%2Fanat-kimchi.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1190815/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1828x1026+0+157/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc8%2F8a%2F7f2bdd53f8d86fe2f2d7fcdc5ec5%2Fanat-kimchi.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f67c119/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1828x1026+0+157/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc8%2F8a%2F7f2bdd53f8d86fe2f2d7fcdc5ec5%2Fanat-kimchi.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Anat Kimchi, a University of Maryland graduate student, was stabbed to death in 2021 near Lower Wacker Drive, where her alleged killer Tony Robinson was living in a tent.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Robinson's jury trial at the George N. Leighton Criminal Courthouse is overseen by Judge John F. Lyke Jr.</p><p>Jones testified Monday that as he saw Robinson stab Kimchi two times in the back of the neck at 401 S. Wacker Drive, he yelled "no" and ran over to try to stop the attack. Jones said Robinson turned to him and said, "You don’t want none of these problems, big bro."</p><p>Jones testified Robinson then ran away toward Lower Wacker. Jones used Kimchi's phone to call 911 and stayed with her until an ambulance and police arrived.</p><p>On June 22, Jones viewed a photo lineup but was unable to identify Robinson. He testified that at the time, he struggled with substance abuse and was "inebriated" when he looked at the photo lineup.</p><p>Jones testified that the next day, he went to confront Robinson at the tent on Lower Wacker where he heard he was living. Jones testified that Robinson came out, but then "his face changed" and he ducked back into the tent and emerged again holding a knife with "a very specific blade." Jones said Robinson said to him, "What are you doing down here, big bro?"</p><p>Jones said he realized Robinson’s photo had been in the photo array he'd seen a day earlier and told police.</p><p>On June 25, he viewed an in-person lineup at Area 3 detective headquarters and identified Robinson as Kimchi's killer.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-7a0000" name="image-7a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f2d15b5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F28%2F9b3c24604b17a11de4cfc8bf4a0d%2Ftavon-jones.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Tavon Jones." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f2d15b5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F28%2F9b3c24604b17a11de4cfc8bf4a0d%2Ftavon-jones.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/124e918/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F28%2F9b3c24604b17a11de4cfc8bf4a0d%2Ftavon-jones.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f2d15b5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F28%2F9b3c24604b17a11de4cfc8bf4a0d%2Ftavon-jones.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Prosecutors called Tavon Jones as a witness in Tony Robinson’s murder trial on Monday. Jones identified Robinson as the man who stabbed grad student Anat Kimchi on June 19, 2021.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>L.D. Chukman/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Kimchi had come to Chicago from Maryland to visit a friend.</p><p>Assistant State’s Attorney Anna Sedelmaier told the jury that Kimchi "wasn't bothering anyone, she wasn't looking for any trouble. She was just walking, looking for a place to read a book."</p><p>Her mother, Chava Kimchi-Sarfaty, told the jury that her daughter "was fun to be with, she loves dogs — she had a dog. She was a bright Ph. D. student."</p><p>"For us, she was our daughter. She called me every day … I miss her so much," said Kimchi-Sarfaty, who lives in North Potomac, Maryland.</p><p>Kimchi's father, Avraham, and brothers, Itamar and Ofer, also attended the trial Monday.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-6b0000" name="module-6b0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h4><b>FAILURE TO TREAT, FAILURE TO PROTECT</b></h4><i>This investigation by the Sun-Times examines how the failures of the mental health system in Chicago have had deadly consequences and looks at possible solutions.</i><br><h5><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/chicago-downtown-attacks-mental-health-system" >Read more stories in the series > </a></h5></div> </div><p>Robinson's tent on Lower Wacker Drive was near where the killing took place.</p><p>Public defenders Robyn Haynes and Ali Ammoura tried to poke holes in Jones's eyewitness testimony, pointing out inconsistencies in his initial description of the offender to police, including that the killer had "sh--y-ass dreads" and was younger and taller than Robinson is.</p><p>Robinson didn't have dreadlocks at the time of his arrest.</p><p>Jones testified that it was a chaotic scene and that he was focused on the victim.</p><p>"I specifically said 'short dreads,' which is referred to in our community as kinky twists," Jones said in explaining the hair discrepancy.</p><p>According to police and court records, Robinson had a history of arrests and bizarre behavior, including telling detectives "he believes people are following him and tracking his location using their cellphones" and that "he sees people looking at their cellphones and has gone up behind them to look over their shoulder to see if they are tracking him."</p><p>Robinson's arrests started in his teens.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" Leighton Criminal Courthouse on Monday. He’s standing trial on murder charges in the stabbing of Anat Kimchi, a graduate student from Maryland.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>L.D. Chukman/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Authorities say that in the days before Kimchi's killing, Robinson struck a woman in the head in the 500 block of South Franklin Street and three days later hit another woman in the head, breaking her nose, and stole her iPhone and cash near Congress Parkway and South Michigan Avenue. That woman, a tourist from St. Louis in town for a Cubs game, had left a hotel to get coffee when she said she heard Robinson say, "Are you following me?" according to police reports.</p><p>On Monday, Robinson, dressed in a light-colored button-down shirt and black pants and wearing a white cap on his head, spent most of the day facing the jury, leaning back in his chair against the wall of the courtroom.</p><p>In a previous interview with the Sun-Times, Kimchi's family mourned the loss of their daughter, a promising student whose research focus was the criminal justice system.</p><p>She had earned two undergraduate degrees and a master's from the University of Maryland in College Park and was posthumously awarded her doctorate degree two weeks after her death.</p><p>Her research focused on which nonviolent offenders were the most likely to abide by conditions of their early release from prison and which were the likeliest to fail.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/04/10/anat-kimchi-downtown-chicago-random-killings-mentally-ill-tony-robinson-lower-wacker-drive-homeless-tikkun-olam-university-maryland-college-park" target="_blank" >A middle child with two brothers</a>, Kimchi was creative as well as analytical, her family says. She liked writing, dancing and making art, and she was an animal lover.</p><p>Her family emigrated from Israel to the United States when she was 8.</p><p>Her high school in Maryland created an award in her honor to recognize outstanding alumni who contribute to society. It’s called the "Anat Kimchi Award for Excellence in <i>Tikkun Olam</i>," which is a Hebrew phrase that means "repairing the world."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div entries.title: Cubs' Seiya Suzuki counsels White Sox' Munetaka Murakami about life in Chicago
entries.summary: <p>ATLANTA — When Japanese slugger Munetaka Murakami signed with the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" >White Sox</a> this past winter, he reached out to Cubs veteran Seiya Suzuki for advice.</p><p>“[It was] nothing really about baseball,’’ Suzuki said through his translator Sunday morning in Texas before the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" target="_blank" >Cubs</a> flew to Atlanta to open a three-game set against the Braves that starts Tuesday. “More about Chicago, you know — where to live, where to eat.”</p><p>Because Murakami would be playing on the South Side, Suzuki recommended finding a place downtown.</p><p>“Nearby, and good security,” he said.</p><p>And did Murakami take his advice?</p><p>“I don’t know where he is,” Suzuki said. “I haven’t seen him.”</p><p>That changes this weekend, when Suzuki and the Cubs will travel to Rate Field for the first round of the Crosstown Series with the Sox. The teams meet again in August at Wrigley Field.</p><p>Murakami has been a revelation in his first MLB season, hitting 15 home runs in his first 38 games, which put him third in the majors ahead of play Monday, one homer behind the Yankees’ Aaron Judge and the Phillies’ Kyle Schwarber. He announced his arrival by homering in each of his first three games, then in five straight games from April 17 to 22.</p><p>Can Murakami Mania be far behind, especially with the Sox flirting with first place in the American League Central?</p><p>“I think it’s impressive,” Suzuki said, “especially because the environment has changed for him. If he was putting up those numbers in Japan, I wouldn’t be surprised. But since coming over to a different country and stadium, and he’s still doing the same thing, that’s impressive.”</p><p>Despite hitting 246 home runs in eight seasons with the Tokyo Yakult Swallows of Nippon Professional Baseball’s Central League, including a career-high 56 homers in 2022, Murakami attracted limited interest when he announced his intentions to play in the United States. The Sox, facing limited competition, were able to sign him to a two-year, $34 million deal. Contrast that with the five-year, $85 million deal that Suzuki signed with the Cubs before the 2022 season.</p><p>Murakami already has exceeded the 14 homers Suzuki hit in 111 games during that first season in Chicago.</p><p>“My first year, you know, all of my teammates, everybody was really nice to me,” Suzuki said. “It was nice to be around.</p><p>“But I think, with the [different] food and everything, I think it wasn’t until my second year that I felt acclimated.”</p><p>Suzuki missed the first dozen games of this season after hurting his right knee attempting to steal second base while playing for Japan in the World Baseball Classic against Venezuela, the eventual champion. The injury was diagnosed as a minor sprain of the posterior cruciate ligament, which runs down the back of the knee, connecting the thigh bone to the shinbone. It’s far less common than an injury to the anterior cruciate ligament, according to the Cleveland Clinic, accounting for about 20% of knee ligament injuries.</p><p>Suzuki has recovered to hit seven home runs through the Cubs’ first 41 games, which puts him on a pace to hit 28 this season, four fewer than last season’s 32. The most he hit in a single season in Japan was 38 in 2021, the year before he signed with the Cubs.</p><p>Another Japanese slugger, third baseman Kazuma Okamoto, also made his big-league debut this season, so far hitting 10 home runs for the Blue Jays. He and Murakami are both ahead of Suzuki and Dodgers designated hitter and pitcher Shohei Ohtani, who was off to a slow start with just six home runs, well behind the pace that led to his major-league-best 54 and 55 home runs in 2024 and 2025, respectively.</p><p>All four players represented Team Japan in the WBC this spring. Is there a rivalry?</p><p>“There’s no real sense of competition,” Suzuki said. “It’s more like you check in on what they’re doing, and it gives you a little motivation. You know they’re working hard, and you want to work hard.”</p><p>All four players spoke frequently to each other then, although not as much recently.</p><p>“Everybody’s doing well,” Suzuki said. “I think it’s a good thing, especially for younger Japanese players that aspire to come to Major League Baseball. I think watching us, they’ll work harder.”</p><p>At 26, Murakami is the youngest of the four. Okamoto turns 30 at the end of June, Ohtani 32 in July and Suzuki 32 a month later.</p><p>Veteran reporter Katsushi Nagao of Kyodo News said Suzuki isn’t really regarded in Japan as a prototypical home-run hitter — and doesn’t see himself that way, either.</p><p>“In the spring,” Nagao said, “I would ask Seiya about Murakami, and he would say, ‘It might take him time to get used to [MLB], but he’s going to hit home runs. But not me.”</p><p>Statistics compiled by MLB’s Baseball Savant suggest Suzuki doesn’t have the classic slugger’s uppercut. Murakami, on the other hand, has an attack angle of 19%, which ranks third in MLB. Okamoto is at 16%, Ohtani 13% (like Judge) and Suzuki 10%.</p><p>“In Japan, they’re comparing Shohei and Murakami because Murakami is at such a hot pace,” Nagao said.</p><p>Nagao noted that in Japan, it’s customary to be deferential to your elders.</p><p>“Seiya said to me, ‘I don’t really check [Murakami’s] stats every day, but he’s a nice follower of me.’ They are close, but not like brothers. I’m sure this weekend, they will be chatting with each other.”</p><p>There is a special pride, Suzuki said, in seeing his countrymen thrive.</p><p>“For me, especially when I came over, there were a lot of [Japanese] right-handed hitters who had difficulty adjusting to the league,” he said. “I feel like I was able to kind of break that wall down, where there are plenty of right-handed hitters from Japan who can come over and produce, like Okamoto.</p><p>“I think in Japan right now, the popularity of baseball is going down a little bit — not as many younger kids watching the games. The guys who are playing here now, myself included, want to increase that interest, not only for the fans but for the players who might want to come over.”</p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" entries.title: Horoscope for Tuesday, May 12, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>Caution! Avoid shopping (except food and gas) and major decisions from 4:45 a.m. until 7:45 p.m. After that, the moon moves from Pisces into Aries.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> A tricky day! You might want to buy something exciting. It's important to know today is a moon alert, which means restrict spending to food and gas. Tomorrow is a great day.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> For most of this day, the moon is in your sign, dancing with Jupiter, making you feel extroverted and eager to make your pitch about whatever interests you. Alas, most of this day is a moon alert. See above. Postpone until tomorrow. (Tomorrow is strong.)</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actress Katharine Hepburn (1907-2003) shares your birthday today. You are methodical, dependable, logical, and tenacious. You're also caring and supportive. This is the end of a nine-year cycle, making it a time to let go of people, places, and things that hold you back. Do some internal and external housecleaning. Focus on being compassionate and loving.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Life and death near Chicago suburbs' booming warehouse hub
entries.summary: <p>It’s a typical weekday afternoon at the intersection of Route 53 and Laraway Road, 40 miles southwest of Chicago, and semi-trucks thunder by in a steady stream.</p><p>They start at the sprawling warehouse complexes on the fringes of Joliet, rumble alongside the fading polyester petals of a roadside memorial for someone who died in a truck crash and roll past the ballfields where youth teams play, carrying goods for Amazon, IKEA, Walmart, Target and Dollar Tree.</p><p>In the next 10 minutes, 150 trucks will pass through the intersection. If you lined them up end to end, they would stretch more than two miles.</p><p>Three decades ago, this area was mostly prairie sprinkled with quiet subdivisions. But the early 2000s ushered in the age of online shopping. Then came the rise of next-day delivery. America’s retailers needed warehouses, fast, and the area outside Chicago — flush with interstates and rail lines — was perfect.</p><p>Few places in the nation have been transformed so completely so quickly. Since 2000, retail giants and developers have erected more than 146 million square feet of warehouse space in the Chicago area — equivalent in size to roughly 1,400 Home Depot stores.</p><p>The warehouses have brought new jobs. Still, people who live nearby say what has happened around them is a cautionary tale for other communities hoping to cash in on the warehouse boom.</p><p>On average, roughly 20,000 trucks pass through Joliet, pop. roughly 150,000 people, every day. Most keep to Interstate 80, but as many as 6,400 — more than five times as many as before the warehouse boom — use local roads and state highways.</p><p>They pummel roads, belch fumes and batter the pavement, contributing to road damage that requires <a class="Link" href="https://8cmbykf0.r.us-east-1.awstrack.me/L0/https:%2F%2Fpublic.powerdms.com%2FIDOT%2Fdocuments%2F3173620/1/0100019e17f7fc19-52c5b68c-222b-4ab2-968a-9b44766be9f2-000000/oINe9KislV3zsNzAMBDkLDlbzds=473" target="_blank" ><u>millions of dollars</u></a> of repairs <a class="Link" href="https://8cmbykf0.r.us-east-1.awstrack.me/L0/https:%2F%2Fidot.illinois.gov%2Fabout-idot%2Fstay-connected%2Fidot-blog%2Fnew--41-billion-multi-year-improvement-program-is-largest-in-sta.html/1/0100019e17f7fc19-52c5b68c-222b-4ab2-968a-9b44766be9f2-000000/42vz53c9tZqG_jZa3Zx00ZTn-AY=473" target="_blank" ><u>paid</u></a> for by local and state governments, according to budget and grant documents.</p><p>And crashes have become more common, according to a New York Times analysis that used satellite imagery, government documents and interviews with people who live in the areas, law enforcement officers and traffic safety experts to identify some of the largest clusters of warehouses in the Chicago area. Then, using state data from January 2014 through December 2024, reporters counted the number of crashes involving trucks that occurred on the surrounding roads.</p><div class="Enhancement" </div> </div><p>The number of truck wrecks on those roads increased by 8% from 2021 to 2024 compared with truck accidents from 2016 to 2019, the four-year period before the COVID-19 pandemic. This was even as the number of crashes involving other types of vehicles on these roads dropped sharply, and truck crashes across the state remained largely flat.</p><p>In recent years, an average of nearly 550 people a year were injured in truck crashes in those neighborhoods, and one person died every month.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" The New York Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Ian Hunnicutt was in line at the grocery store in the Will County town of Manhattan, southwest of Chicago, on a Monday evening last October, picking up frozen pizza and sushi rolls for dinner for his 13-year-old twin boys when his wife called.</p><p>She was hysterical. Something had happened to the kids, Ryder and Chance, while they were biking to the library. Find them, she told her husband through sobs.</p><p>He rushed through Manhattan, checking their usual haunts. Then, he rounded a bend and went around a police barricade.</p><p>He found Ryder holding a single Nike shoe — Chance’s shoe. It had flown off when he was struck and killed by a semi-truck while riding his bike.</p><p>“This is what Ryder was holding when my life changed,” Hunnicutt said, holding up the sandal. “When my world fell apart.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-060000" name="image-060000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7b22da3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1079x606+0+57/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcc%2Fc1%2F4121738d4cacb6579949b2eda5f7%2F1778529780554.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/512e07b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1079x606+0+57/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcc%2Fc1%2F4121738d4cacb6579949b2eda5f7%2F1778529780554.jpeg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d53a9c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1079x606+0+57/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcc%2Fc1%2F4121738d4cacb6579949b2eda5f7%2F1778529780554.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="This makeshift memorial was put up at the intersection where Chance Hunnicutt was killed. He can be seen in the middle of the photo placed in the "In loving memory" memorial." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d53a9c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1079x606+0+57/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcc%2Fc1%2F4121738d4cacb6579949b2eda5f7%2F1778529780554.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/92316cf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1079x606+0+57/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcc%2Fc1%2F4121738d4cacb6579949b2eda5f7%2F1778529780554.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d53a9c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1079x606+0+57/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcc%2Fc1%2F4121738d4cacb6579949b2eda5f7%2F1778529780554.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>This makeshift memorial was put up at the intersection where Chance Hunnicutt was killed. He can be seen in the middle of the photo placed in the “In loving memory” memorial.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Cam Rodriguez </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>The warehouse boom</h3><p>Around the country,<b> </b>warehouses have popped up outside major cities, where land tends to be cheaper. Exceptionally dense areas include Ontario, Calif.,once dotted with dairy farms but now dominated by warehouses, and a stretch of Interstate 35 northwest of Dallas lined with distribution centers.</p><p>But Chicago is in a different league. The warehouse boom in the Chicago suburbs took off in earnest in the early 2000s with the construction of the CenterPoint Intermodal Center, the largest inland port in North America, where trucks and trains swap goods. It sits just outside Elwood, a Will County town of roughly 2,200 residents. In 2015, Amazon opened its first Illinois warehouse, in Joliet.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c70000" name="image-c70000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9cc8d37/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+211/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2Fb8%2F6454732d46b889b9060214a3b96f%2Fnyt-truckswarehousesil-0158.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7f48edc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+211/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2Fb8%2F6454732d46b889b9060214a3b96f%2Fnyt-truckswarehousesil-0158.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f536c9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+211/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2Fb8%2F6454732d46b889b9060214a3b96f%2Fnyt-truckswarehousesil-0158.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Intermodal container handlers at the CenterPoint Intermodal Center near Elwood. The facility is a massive freight hub where goods are transferred between rail and truck, serving as a key link in national and international supply chains. It is part of one of the largest inland ports in the United States, drawing significant truck and train traffic to the surrounding area, which includes several rural small towns. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f536c9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+211/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2Fb8%2F6454732d46b889b9060214a3b96f%2Fnyt-truckswarehousesil-0158.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/857faf8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+211/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2Fb8%2F6454732d46b889b9060214a3b96f%2Fnyt-truckswarehousesil-0158.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f536c9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+211/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2Fb8%2F6454732d46b889b9060214a3b96f%2Fnyt-truckswarehousesil-0158.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Intermodal container handlers at the CenterPoint Intermodal Center near Elwood. The facility is a massive freight hub where goods are transferred between rail and truck, serving as a key link in national and international supply chains. It is part of one of the largest inland ports in the United States, drawing significant truck and train traffic to the surrounding area, which includes several rural small towns. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Jamie Kelter Davis / The New York Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p><a class="Link" href="https://8cmbykf0.r.us-east-1.awstrack.me/L0/https:%2F%2Fwww.nytimes.com%2F2024%2F07%2F24%2Fclimate%2Fe-commerce-truck-pollution.html/1/0100019e17f7fc19-52c5b68c-222b-4ab2-968a-9b44766be9f2-000000/7wMk35v53lmX6EH4a5lUPBCRmDQ=473" target="_blank" ><u>For a study about warehouses and pollution,</u></a> researcher Gaige Kerr examined real estate listings from the commercial data company CoStar and determined that there were roughly 6,800 warehouses in the Chicago area as of 2022. Their combined square footage eclipsed that of warehouse space in the Los Angeles metropolitan area — home to the nation’s two largest shipping ports — by 13%.</p><p>Of the nation’s 25 largest metro areas, Chicago had the most warehouse square footage per person, Kerr found.</p><p>According to 2024 data, the latest available from the Illinois Department of Employment Security, warehousing and transportation was the largest sector in Will County, employing <a class="Link" href="https://8cmbykf0.r.us-east-1.awstrack.me/L0/https:%2F%2Fides.illinois.gov%2Fcontent%2Fdam%2Fsoi%2Fen%2Fweb%2Fides%2Flabor_market_information%2Fwhere_workers_work%2F2024.pdf/1/0100019e17f7fc19-52c5b68c-222b-4ab2-968a-9b44766be9f2-000000/5pbN0nvKxm_P6a0rMTWvTIUz1l4=473" target="_blank" ><u>nearly 37,000 people</u></a>. Amazon is Will County’s largest employer.</p><p>“I think people have to look at this as an economic advantage,” said Mark Denzler, president and chief executive officer of the Illinois Manufacturers’ Association.</p><p>But, for years, people living in the area have complained of miserable conditions. Some said they have given up on the promises from government officials that the boom would bring a renaissance of restaurants and shopping centers.</p><p>Some still drive two or three towns away for groceries even though they are surrounded by warehouses stocked with the things they need. In certain places, retailers and developers — many that get tax incentives from the state — have gobbled up much of the usable land and pushed up property values to levels that once would have been unimaginable.</p><p>And many of the new jobs that have been created are part-time or low paying. In 2024, the majority of full-time employees at Amazon’s Joliet facility made an average yearly salary shy of $34,000, according to a report filed with the state.</p><p>Meanwhile, developers have continued to build.</p><p>This year, a 1.1 million-square-foot logistics facility is scheduled to open next to the only school in Elwood, just over a quarter mile from home plate of the school’s baseball field. Thousands of trucks could pass by each day.</p><h3>Trucks prompt ‘Die-80’ nickname</h3><p>Unsurprisingly, the increase in traffic has led to more crashes.</p><p>Madison Rose Frost was 8 years old when she was killed in March 2014 after her family’s minivan was struck by a truck on Route 53. Ten years later, Robert Roach Jr., 32, died a few miles from Madison’s roadside memorial following a collision with a stalled semi.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c10000" name="image-c10000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0599e3c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2214x1243+0+68/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F99%2F64%2F40abb745488bb2371c6783b6d840%2Fscreenshot-2026-05-11-at-3-55-00-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/59cb600/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2214x1243+0+68/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F99%2F64%2F40abb745488bb2371c6783b6d840%2Fscreenshot-2026-05-11-at-3-55-00-pm.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c24e48/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2214x1243+0+68/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F99%2F64%2F40abb745488bb2371c6783b6d840%2Fscreenshot-2026-05-11-at-3-55-00-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A roadside memorial was erected near where Madison Rose Frost, 8, was struck and killed by a semi-truck. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c24e48/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2214x1243+0+68/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F99%2F64%2F40abb745488bb2371c6783b6d840%2Fscreenshot-2026-05-11-at-3-55-00-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b5ae4ca/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2214x1243+0+68/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F99%2F64%2F40abb745488bb2371c6783b6d840%2Fscreenshot-2026-05-11-at-3-55-00-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c24e48/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2214x1243+0+68/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F99%2F64%2F40abb745488bb2371c6783b6d840%2Fscreenshot-2026-05-11-at-3-55-00-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A roadside memorial was erected near where Madison Rose Frost, 8, was struck and killed by a semi-truck. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Jamie Kelter Davis / The New York Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Such stories aren’t uncommon in the area of the warehouse boom. A truck driver, unaware that a vehicle had become lodged beneath his trailer, barreled down the Tri-State Tollway, dragging the car — and the woman inside — with it. Miraculously, she survived. A man was hit and killed in downtown Joliet minutes after he had been released from jail.</p><p>Sara Wittchen said she worries about crashes, something she never did while growing up in Joliet.</p><p>“Fatal accidents were reserved for 2 a.m. on the highway with a drunk driver,” Wittchen said. “It wasn’t Grandma Lucy in an Enclave going to Target.”</p><p>In the span of two months last fall in Will County, at least seven people died in accidents involving trucks. So far this year, at least 60 people have been injured in crashes, and two have died.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-640000" name="image-640000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/13d808c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/898x504+0+439/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe7%2F00%2F26de505d4926936aef603fbd9caf%2Fbefore-after-2x3-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d3f79b8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/898x504+0+439/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe7%2F00%2F26de505d4926936aef603fbd9caf%2Fbefore-after-2x3-1.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6d2b0ad/2147483647/strip/true/crop/898x504+0+439/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe7%2F00%2F26de505d4926936aef603fbd9caf%2Fbefore-after-2x3-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="In 2002 (images at left from the U.S. Geological Survey), the far outskirts of the Chicago suburbs were mostly farmland. Today (images at right, from Vexcel), the growing demand for next-day delivery has seen warehouses dominate the landscape.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Daniel Wood / The New York Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The crashes aren’t always the fault of the truck driver. But the hazards tend to be greater for the drivers in smaller vehicles.</p><p>And people have become increasingly wary of driving around this area.</p><p>Some even have a nickname for the stretch of I-80 that passes through Joliet: “Die-80.” More than 60,000 people have joined a Facebook group called “I-80 and I-55 Corridor DAILY DEATH TRAP.”</p><p>It’s where Ryan Hart was killed in 2020, when a truck rear-ended his camper at the height of morning rush hour. The camper and four other vehicles were engulfed in flames. Another driver also died.</p><p>Bob Ilibasic, a terminal manager at a trucking company, was a close friend of Hart.</p><p>“He’s never going to see the grandkids,” Ilibasic said. “All for a truck that didn’t stop.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ae0000" name="image-ae0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/590c1c9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+263/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9f%2F55%2Fafbb5edd43099c1ba53a549e60d8%2Fnyt-truckwarehouse2-096.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2dd5e24/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+263/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9f%2F55%2Fafbb5edd43099c1ba53a549e60d8%2Fnyt-truckwarehouse2-096.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9b7e218/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+263/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9f%2F55%2Fafbb5edd43099c1ba53a549e60d8%2Fnyt-truckwarehouse2-096.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Bob Ilibasic in a parking lot in Joliet where some of his truck trailers are parked before drivers get them up so they can pick up loads from warehouses and nearby intermodal stations. Ilibasic is terminal manager for American Marine Express, managing a fleet of truck drivers." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9b7e218/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+263/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9f%2F55%2Fafbb5edd43099c1ba53a549e60d8%2Fnyt-truckwarehouse2-096.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/04d22ce/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+263/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9f%2F55%2Fafbb5edd43099c1ba53a549e60d8%2Fnyt-truckwarehouse2-096.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9b7e218/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+263/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9f%2F55%2Fafbb5edd43099c1ba53a549e60d8%2Fnyt-truckwarehouse2-096.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Bob Ilibasic was friends with Ryan Hart, who was killed in 2020 when a semi rear-ended his camper at the height of morning rush hour. Ilibasic is terminal manager for American Marine Express, managing a fleet of truck drivers. Of his friend, he says: “He’s never going to see the grandkids. All for a truck that didn’t stop.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Jamie Kelter Davis / The New York Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Residents fight back</h3><p>Data from the Illinois Department of Transportation shows that the most hazardous places are the interstates, where truck crashes that cause traffic backups are common. But collisions occur on residential roads, too, even those that ban or limit trucks.</p><p>Trucks sometimes end up on these roads — and closer to residential areas — when the interstates get backed up. State data shows that more than 200 crashes from 2014 through 2024 involved damage to homes, playgrounds and schools. They flattened fences, tore through yards and smashed mailboxes.</p><p>Don Schaefer, president of the Mid-West Truckers Association, said drivers are forced onto a smaller number of routes as more municipalities try to restrict the use of local roads.</p><p>“There simply aren’t enough truck routes,” he said.</p><p>Many trucking companies don’t build in enough time for delays, said Zach Cahalan, executive director of the Truck Safety Coalition, an organization focused on reducing the number of deaths and injuries from truck crashes. Some truckers might feel pressure to make up lost time, he said.</p><p>“Truck drivers pay the cost,” Cahalan said. “And, of course, crash victims.”</p><p>Several police departments have added truck enforcement units to ticket drivers who violate weight and size rules. <a class="Link" href="https://8cmbykf0.r.us-east-1.awstrack.me/L0/https:%2F%2Fpatch.com%2Fillinois%2Fjoliet%2Fjoliet-approves-extension-commercial-truck-enforcement-efforts/1/0100019e17f7fc19-52c5b68c-222b-4ab2-968a-9b44766be9f2-000000/7Zn6UI__Hve7sKzs5WkiiE8WtdA=473" target="_blank" ><u>Joliet’s unit brought in $2.2 million in fines</u></a> in its first 15 months, though city officials said many truck drivers have continued to break the rules.</p><p>Joliet is trying to divert trucks away from local roads, according to a city government official who said in a written statement: “Through recent agreements tied to major logistics developments,” the city “has required the use of a closed-loop truck network specifically designed to keep trucks off neighborhood streets.”</p><p>But community residents are skeptical that the designated loop will curb traffic alt="A "no trucks" sign by a park in Elwood, which is home to part of the CenterPoint Intermodal Center — one of the nation’s largest inland ports, where cargo is transferred between trains and trucks, contributing to the heavy industrial traffic that moves through the area. Some communities have taken steps to ban or limit semi-trucks on local roads, putting up signs and ticketing and fining truckers." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c618478/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+211/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1a%2F3a%2F36e8975a49ea8ef1204226185950%2Fnyt-truckswarehousesil-01331.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6998a4b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+211/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1a%2F3a%2F36e8975a49ea8ef1204226185950%2Fnyt-truckswarehousesil-01331.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c618478/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+211/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1a%2F3a%2F36e8975a49ea8ef1204226185950%2Fnyt-truckswarehousesil-01331.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A “no trucks” sign by a park in Elwood, which is home to part of the CenterPoint Intermodal Center — one of the nation’s largest inland ports, where cargo is transferred between trains and trucks, contributing to the heavy industrial traffic that moves through the area. Some communities have taken steps to ban or limit semi-trucks on local roads, putting up signs and ticketing and fining truckers.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Jamie Kelter Davis / The New York Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Some residents and local governments have taken steps to stop more development or try to reduce traffic near residential areas, with mixed results.</p><p>After Delilah LeGrett moved, in part because trucks would rumble past her front door, she found out that NorthPoint, a Kansas City-based developer, wanted to move in, too. Her house is along the edge of a proposed logistics complex in Will County that, if completed, would be five<b> </b>times the size of Midway Airport.</p><p>The complex has faced lawsuits filed by, among others, LeGrett and her neighbors, locking up development and construction. But a settlement last year ironed out truck-route access, and the pending outcome of suits this year means the facility could soon break ground.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-040000" name="image-040000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a54dfd6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+263/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fca%2F5d%2F9bd899a14593894952372de38a8a%2Fnyt-truckwarehouse2-041.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/800c4f0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+263/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fca%2F5d%2F9bd899a14593894952372de38a8a%2Fnyt-truckwarehouse2-041.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2bd21f9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+263/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fca%2F5d%2F9bd899a14593894952372de38a8a%2Fnyt-truckwarehouse2-041.jpg"
